content_html:
Week 1:
What is data?

MDA10012: Communicating with Data


Dr Kyle Moore

  Lecture Overview






               What is data?



               Why study data? How to study
               data?


               Thinking about data and concepts











What is data?                                                                                        2

   What is data?






        At its most basic level, data are
        measurements or observations
        collected as information for
        reference or analysis (Australian
        Bureau of Statistics, n.d.)
        Data can be quantitative (i.e.
        numerical) or qualitative (i.e.
        observations)
        It can come from a range of
        sources in a variety of forms
        Data can also have a more
        colloquial meaning too, i.e. mobile
        data plans


What is data?                                                                                                                      3

A brief history of
data



     The word data has Latin roots, meaning “to
     give” or “thing given”
     Adopted new meaning in 1890’s –      numerical
     facts collected for future reference
     In 1940’s – referred to computational
     information
     See also emergence of ‘database’ and ‘data
     processing’










                                                                                                                  4

   Types of data






     Quantitative and Qualitative –      numerical and non-numerical
     data
     Structured, semi-structured, and unstructured –          degrees in
     which data is stored or organised
     Captured, exhaust, transient, derived –        how data is
     generated
     Primary, Secondary, Tertiary –      data generated or used in
     research settings
     Indexical and attribute data -     can data be used as an
     identifier?
     Metadata –    data about data




Source: Kitchen (2014)                                                                                                          5

   Data and
   Knowledge

The Data-Information-Knowledge-Wisdom (DIKW)
pyramid illustrates the progression of raw data to valuable
insights.
•  Wisdom -   well informed decisions and effective action based
   on understanding of the underlying knowledge.
•  Knowledge -    the result of analyzing  and interpreting
   information to uncover patterns, trends, and relationships.
•  Information is organized, structured, and contextualized data.
•  Data refers to raw, unprocessed facts and figures without
   context.










What is data?                                                                                                                           6

 Data and
 Knowledge































What is data?                                                 7

   Raw Data




     We need to be careful with the term                               “Raw data is both an oxymoron and
     ‘raw data’                                                        a bad idea; to the contrary, data
     What do we mean by raw data?                                      should be cooked with care.”
                                                                       (Gitelman, 2013)
     We mean data that has been
     collected and unprocessed.
     This does not always equal fact
     Gitelman   (2013) discusses how data
     is ‘cooked’ (as opposed to raw) and
     how we need to take care when
     collecting, processing, and
     understanding data







What is data?                                                                                                                  8

   Big Data






         Big data are data sets so large or complex
         they need specific software to process
         It is big in the following sense:
         •  volume (consisting of enormous
            quantities of data);
         •  velocity (created in real-time) and;
         •  variety (being structured, semi-structured
            and unstructured).
         (Kitchin  and McArdle,     2016)







What is data?                                                                                                                       9

  Big vs Small Data



  Table 1. Comparing small and Big Data.
                              Small data                  Big Data
  Volume                      Limited to large            Very large
  Velocity                    Slow, freeze-framed/        Fast, continuous
                              bundled
  Variety                     Limited to wide             Wide
  Exhaustivity                Samples                     Entire populations
  Resolution and              Course and weak to          Tight and strong
  indexicality                tight and strong
  Relationality               Weak to strong              Strong
  Extensionality and          Low to middling             High
  scalability




Source: Kitchin and McArdle (2016)                                                                                    10

   Why study data?






        These topics will be covered more in future
        weeks, but it’s worth acknowledging them
        now.
        1)  Data is everywhere
        2)  Data is ‘valuable’
        3)  Data literacy skills are becoming
            increasingly essential to modern work
        Data has been flagged as ‘the new oil’, an
        essential valuable commodity essential to
        digital technologies (Toonders, 2014)






What is data?                                                                                                               11

   How to study
   data?



        Various fields of studies use data
        Data analytics, data science, all in revolve
        around analysing data
        Extracting knowledge and insights from data
        sets
        Insights turned into actions
        Focus on predictive analysis
        Various other fields use data too –      specific
        data for medical research, geography, even
        history –  all use the term ‘data’ in unique
        and specific, but similar, ways




What is data?                                                                                                                  12

  A media studies
  approach to data



                  Less concerned with the use of                        This unit combines these two
                  data as information or facts                          elements –   critical data studies
                  Less concerned with predictive                        with communication essentials –
                  analysis and insights                                 to provide both theoretical and
                                                                        practical understanding of data at
                                                                        a first-year level

                  More concerned about the power
                  of data
                  More concerned with how to
                  communicate with and about data







What is data?                                                                                                        13

  Summary






        Data is not new
        Data is always social
        Develop a critical approach to data
        Develop communication essentials

















What is data?                                                                                                        14

   References

        Gitelman, L. (2013). ‘Raw Data’ Is an Oxymoron
        (1st edition). The MIT Press.
        Kitchin, R. (2014). Chapter 1 | Conceptualising
        Data. In The Data Revolution: Big Data, Open Data,
        Data Infrastructures &Their Consequences. SAGE
        Publications Ltd.
        https://doi.org/10.4135/9781473909472
        Kitchin, R., & McArdle, G. (2016). What makes Big
        Data, Big Data? Exploring the ontological
        characteristics of 26 datasets. Big Data & Society,
        3(1), 2053951716631130.
        https://doi.org/10.1177/2053951716631130
        Toonders, J. (n.d.). Data Is the New Oil of the Digital
        Economy | WIRED. Retrieved 22 February 2024,
        from https://www.wired.com/insights/2014/07/data-
        new-oil-digital-economy/
